preamble: The transformation of an alveolar nasal consonant /n/ into a voiceless palatal plosive /c/ represents a complex sound change involving multiple phonetic processes. This shift moves the articulation point from the alveolar ridge to the hard palate, eliminates nasality, and changes the manner of articulation from a continuous nasal sound to a sudden, voiceless stop. While less common than other forms of palatalization or denasalization, this specific shift is attested in certain linguistic contexts.
phoneticEffects: This phonetic transformation encompasses several key effects: palatalization (shift of articulation to the palate), denasalization (loss of nasal airflow), fortition (change from nasal to oral plosive), and devoicing (loss of vocal fold vibration)

examples.note: The alveolar nasal /n/ became a voiceless palatal plosive /c/ before the vowel /i/, particularly when /i/ was a new morphological element or its initial sound.

sources: Michelson, T. (1913). Notes on the Fox Indians. American Anthropologist, 15(3), 470-471.
Michelson, T. (1914). The Linguistic Position of the Algonquian Tribes. Journal of the Washington Academy of Sciences, 4(15), 402-404.
